speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape 4, Bernard Schuster, December 28, 1995. Did you at any time run into other people also hiding, as you were, in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] cousins, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Solomon Schleime Schuster and his brother Elichaim and his brother Maishe, all three of them were hiding in the same area where we were hiding. And on several occasions I met with the youngest of-- Maishe He's the guy who's still alive in Mexico City, owns a travel agency there. He would go out begging for food or we would arrange to meet at a certain place in the middle of the night. Now, he knew where we hiding at Dekar's. But he never told us where they were hiding. And the reason was that he said, look, what you don't know, you cannot give away. If you are captured by the Germans, they will torture you until you give us away, so if you don't know where we are, you can't tell them where we are. So he never told us where they were hiding, even though we were a very short distance from each other, neighbors practically.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you were discovered in your first bunker. Could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One day a farmer and his wife came by. And we could never tell. I think they were chopping wood or picking berries or whatever. But once they saw us and saw the bunker open, we felt that we could no longer use that, because chances were good that they were going to tell someone. You know what I did not describe yet, and that is not really my own personal story, but related to my family and in a way related to me, my cousins Schleime and Elichaim and Maishe survived and managed to get to the forest even though they were-- they went into town to be deported during the liquidation. And what had happened there is that they were all gathered into town. They waited for hours and hours. Then the Germans produced dozens of sleds, because it was winter and during the winter months they would go by horse and sled. And they packed in as many Jews as they could in each sled. And eventually, they drove them to the next town, which is [POLISH]. And it's on the map that I gave you. And that was a few miles away. Normally, it would take about an hour and a half, two hours to get to [POLISH]. They dragged it out to nine hours. The people were freezing. Children were begging for a piece of bread or a drink of water. Many of them died during that nine-hour trip. And eventually they were brought to [POLISH]. They were packed into cattle cars so tight that they couldn't even breathe. And they-- the doors were locked and they were being transported to Treblinka. That was the camp where Jasionowka was taken to, and eventually on to Auschwitz. My uncle, Mordche, who was Schleime's and Elichaim's and Maishe's father, said to them-- he made a little speech in the car. And he said, kinderlach, children, we're all going to die. You have nothing to lose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see if we can't tear a hole-- there was a little window at the top of the cattle car with metal grates. Let's see if we can tear  this out and enlarge the hole a little with our hands. And we will push all the young people out. Jump while the train is moving. Maybe you'll survive. At least you'll have a chance. Jump. So they actually created a stepladder out of bodies, because some people had died. And they crawled on those bodies. And they were lifted out and they jumped. Some survived and others were killed. My brother Meyer was killed when jumping.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schleime and Elichaim and Maishe survived. So did a number of others. They ended up in the forests. And they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they knew where we were in the forest. And so occasionally, we would meet them. But that's what happened with those who went to town and who were deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any fighting or partisan activity in your part of the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was activity in-- in the woods. Unfortunately, the Polish partisans would not let us participate in any way. Later on, there were Russian partisans who came through that area. And they were blowing up German trains and giving the Germans a real rough time. But that was late. By that time, we were pretty well set hiding the way we did and we did not join the partisans in any formal way, although we met with them on occasion. The Russian partisans were helpful. The Polish partisans were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive any news of the events of the war while you were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, occasionally a farmer would tell us that, don't you worry. The Germans are losing the war. And in fact, the Russians are already on Polish soil again, driving the Germans back. And it won't be long now before you will be liberated, so don't you worry about that. And that-- that gave us some courage and gave us some help in surviving. In early part of 1945, the Russian army actually drove the Germans out. And we were free to go wherever we wanted to. At that point, I did not know that anyone from my family had survived. I did not know that my two sisters and niece and Feitche's mother-in-law were hiding with a farmer not too far from where I was. I never knew that. Nor did I know that my brother Maishe was alive. He too was being hidden by a farmer, who-- and Maishe actually spoke a very good Polish and looked like a Polish young man. And the farmer passed him off as a Pole and he survived as a member of that family. So when we came back to town, to Jasionowka, after the Russian army marched in, to amazement and surprise, I found that I had two sisters, one brother, and one niece who survived out of the whole family, plus my cousins, Schleime and Elichaim and Maishe and some friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back for a moment to the-- your liberation. Could you describe the moment when you realized?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was really a non-event event, because we were in the forest at the time and didn't even know that the Germans were no longer there and the Russians were now in control of that area. We might have been there maybe for a day or two beyond liberation, not even realizing that we were already liberated. But eventually, of course, I think we came back to Dekar's house, and we found out, yes, you are free people now. You can go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went back to town. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were about-- Jasionowka was extremely lucky. Many towns had no survivors at all, some of them, one or two. Elie Wiesel, I believe only one or two other people survived from his hometown. Many towns had not a single person survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jasionowka, actually about 125 people survived. And the reason was that Jasionowka was one of the last towns to be liquidated. All the other towns were liquidated months and weeks before Jasionowka. Only Bialystok and Jasionowka remained. And so by that time, we had no illusions. We had no hope. And we prepared better. Most families provided for some hiding places, decided to run into the forests, and so about 125 survived. We all came back to Jasionowka. My family, even though our house was still there, we decided we would live with my cousins in the same house that my cousin Schleime owned, which was a little further out of town still, at the end of Kolonia [INAUDIBLE]. They also had a tannery. All of Jasionowka were tanneries. So my cousin Schleime had a tannery and a large wooden house. And we decided we would all live in his house for the time being, until we decide what to do and where to go. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We started actually producing a little leather, which we would sell in order to get some food. And one of my cousins, the youngest, Maishe, the one who is now in Mexico City, he became a high officer in the Russian army, actually. And he had an office in Bialystok. And he's a very bright man. He speaks something like eight languages fluently. And he became an officer in the Russian army. And he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the other two brothers lived in Jasionowka with us in the same house. And one man who lost both of his legs-- they froze and they had to be amputated-- Yankel Dunska, he also lived in that house. He was a friend of Schleime's. And a couple other people. One Thursday afternoon-- now, by that time, we started hearing rumors that the Polish people formed an organization called AK. And they were dedicated to finishing off killing every Jew that survived. And they were going from town to town attacking Jews and slaughtering them in cold blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what was the month of 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably I would say maybe March or April of 1945, after we were liberated. And one afternoon-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had on the windows at Schleime's house outside shutters, wooden shutters that we would close and then tie in from the inside. And we knew that they were going to come and kill us, so at that time, we decided we would fight. And we accumulated a little arsenal of arms, a couple of rifles, a couple of pistols, a few hand grenades and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get these?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was no problem. Poland was covered with ammunition. Armies coming and going and losing and winning, you could get all of the arms you wanted for very little money. So we accumulated quite a bit of arms. And it was a Thursday afternoon. My cousin Elichaim decided to go out to a neighbor to hire a wagon to take some leather to Bialystok. And in the house were my cousin Schleime, my sisters Feitche and Baile and Roske, and a couple other people from the town, and Yankel Dunska without his legs. And it was not quite dark. It was sort of maybe 6 o'clock in the afternoon, almost dark when-- the doors would be locked by the way-- we heard a knocking on the door. And we were all sitting in the dining room drinking tea or sitting around the table.  And whenever we went to open the door, we would always take a gun with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother Maishe went to the door with a pistol in his hand to inquire who it was. We though maybe it was Elichaim coming back, because he had gone out to hire a wagon. And they said, open up, in Polish. And Maishe said, who is it? And they said, we are neighbors and friends. Now open up! So we knew, of course, what was going on. And Maishe ran back into the dining room and said, we are being attacked. Quick. Now in those houses in Poland, our basements were underneath the houses. And to get down there, you would go down through a-- you would open a trapdoor. And there would be a staircase leading down into the basement. So we opened the trapdoor. And we told all of the women and children to go into the basement. And my brother-in-law Schleime fainted from being scared, so we took him down to the basement.

speaker: interviewee
text: And upstairs only my brother Maishe and I and Yankel Dunska, the man without the legs, remained. And just as we pushed everybody down the basement, they opened fire from machine guns and rifles from all directions. They had set up machine guns from all directions. And it was like a hail of bullets. Now, that was Thursday evening, and my sister Baile was baking bread and challah for the Shabbat. So the whole kitchen was covered with metal-- what do you bake challah on-- metal--

speaker: interviewer
text: Baking trays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baking trays of challahs and breads. And the bullets were hitting those trays and challah was flying all over the kitchen. And it was just horrible, like a hail of bullets. Well, we let them fire. And when they stopped, we responded with fire from our side to show them that we were armed and that they were not going to have an easy time of it. And this went on. They would fire and when they would stop, we would open fire to show them that we were alive. And eventually we heard them give a command to-- for one of them to come and set the house on fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at that point my brother Maishe and I-- by the way, Maishe and I were fighting, firing, and Yankel was supplying us with bullets. We had drawerfuls of ammunition. And he was supplying us with ammunition.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that point, we decided we better do something. So we crawled up to the attic of the house. And the way the houses were built, these wooden houses, they had overhangs where you could see down. The roof was wider than the house and there was a space. You could see down. And when they came up to set the house on fire, we threw out a  grenade at them. And evidently, we killed one, at which point-- and by that time, hours had gone by-- at which point they took their dead and their wounded and they left. And they went to a farmer and asked him for some wagons to carry their dead. And he refused to give them a wagon and they shot him. So we knew what happened. We also found out that the Jews who lived in other parts of the town tried to come to our help. But they set up roadblocks far away. And they wouldn't let anybody get by. They were shooting at them so that nobody could come to our aid. So this is how we fought half of the night. And the next morning my cousin from Bialystok, Maishe, came with a group of Russian soldiers in trucks. And the whole Jewish population left that day, the day after the attack. And that was the last day I saw Jasionowka. We knew that we could never go back again. And then we went to Bialystok, where my cousin actually got us an apartment above a police station so that we would be safe. And the family stayed there. But we decided that we could never live in Poland again, there would never be a life for us in Poland. We couldn't live-- even if they stopped killing us, we couldn't live on the graves of our beloved. We didn't know where they were buried. They were buried here, there, and everywhere, wherever they were shot and killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided that my brother Maishe and I would be the first ones to leave and to go and see if there is  a way to get out of Poland and go to Israel, to Palestine at that time. So more or less on foot and by truck and on trains, whatever we could get a hold of, we went from country to country. We ended up going through Czechoslovakia and Hungary and Austria. And eventually we ended up in Italy, where the Israeli Haganah sent in people to help and take people to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: You might recall that England did not allow movement of Jews to Israel at that time. That was the days-- those were the days of-- of the ship exodus. And eventually, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my brother stayed in Italy. We settled in displaced persons' camps. There were several of them. One of them was-- they were all in southern Italy. Italy is shaped like a boot. And the camp where we were was right here at the heel of the-- the heel of the boot, called Santa Cesarea. There was also another camp a couple miles away, Santa Maria di Leuca. And that's where they kept a lot of displaced people who were waiting to go to Israel if they ever could. My brother was there for quite a while. In fact, his Italian is excellent. I learned Italian, but not that well, because I didn't stay full-time. I went back, actually, a couple of times back to Poland to help them bring other people out, even though I was only about, I don't know, 16, 17 years old. I was working for the Haganah at the time. And in addition, I was studying Hebrew. And I actually have a diploma-- in fact, I have it right here-- from-- from the Israeli group who organized-- who were in charge of the displaced person's camp. I have a diploma to teach Hebrew. I finished the seminar there. I also was studying art, painting. And in the meantime, I also traveled back to Poland several times and brought out other people. At one time, I was leading a group of about 40 Jews towards Italy. And we were going through Germany. And in Germany, I met up with Sholom Bailach. Now, Bailach, two of his brothers married two of my sisters. And he was about 10 years older than I. And he-- in 1940, he was drafted into the Russian army and he became a pilot. And he always used to play with me. He always loved me. To this day, we are very close. He lives in Montreal now. And he was in Germany at the time, in Feldafing. And I was traveling with a group on a train through Germany. And he met me and he insisted that I get off and that I meet his bride. He had-- he had just married a lovely blonde young woman, Sally, who is still his wife in Montreal, and who makes the best kreplach in the whole world. But in any event, so he insisted that I spend a day or two with them. And he said he would drive me and I would catch up with the train, because the trains would go a little ways and stop and go a little ways, and I would catch up with my train and continue leading. So I spent a day or two with him. And when we caught up with the train a couple days later, we found that the train was in a collision. And a couple of the people from my hometown that were part of the group I was leading were killed. In fact, I have a picture of one of them in the album there. Two of his sons survived and are in Israel now, Poplovsky, David and [INAUDIBLE] Poplovksy. But the father was killed in the train wreck. And had I been on that train, I might have been killed as well. But because I met up with Sholom and he took me off the train, I avoided that collision. Eventually, I ended up being shot. We were crossing a border illegally at night and some soldiers fired. And the bullet hit me right here through my hand and my groin. And I ended up in a hospital in Italy. And I have pictures of that also. And while in the hospital in Italy, I found out that I had an aunt in Rochester, New York, Mary Kaufman, my mother's sister, who found out about me through an organization that was helping people locate relatives. And she offered to buy me a ticket and bring me to Rochester. Now, I hesitated to leave, because my brother couldn't come with me. The United States was not taking in refugees either, but they made an exception. They decided to take in youngsters under 18 years of age. And I was under 18 years of age, so I could come here. My brother and my sisters could not.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister Baile ended up going to England. And from England, she ended up in Australia with my niece Roske. My sister Feitche ended up-- she married Schleime after her first husband was killed. And Schleime had a sister in Mexico, so they were able to get to Mexico. So she ended up in Guadalajara, Mexico with Schleime as did Elichaim and Maishe, my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Maishe eventually ended up going to Australia also. My sister, once she settled there, was able to get him over there. So I was the only one who managed to get to the United States. And my family was spread all over the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the DP camp altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left-- I came there in-- sometimes in early '45. And I left-- I came to Rochester on May 22, 1947. I left on May 12, 1947 from Naples on a little boat called And of course, I did not speak a single word of English. Even though I had knowledge of a number of languages, English was not one of them. We did not speak English in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my first few words of English I learned on the boat when they would announce in Italian and then in English, attention, attention, the correct time is such and such, and the first serving of breakfast is now being served. So, you know, you had to eat, so you learned to understand what they were saying. And I came to Rochester in-- on May 22nd, 1947 I came to New York and then a few days later, to Rochester. How were you received in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my aunt Mary Kaufman and her husband received me wonderfully. They had no sons of their own, just one daughter, Bernice. And they took me in and they wanted to treat me like a son. Now, my uncle owned a slaughterhouse. He was the King of Lambs, Kaufman Lambs on Buffalo Road, a large slaughterhouse. And my aunt and uncle treated me very well. They got a teacher to teach me a few words of English. They gave me a few private lessons. And it was the summer, June, when I got to Rochester. And after about a month, they sat down with me and my aunt said to me in Yiddish, Berl, we love you.  You're a wonderful young man. And Willie likes you. His name was Willie Kaufman. And we'd like you to come into the business. We'll teach you how to drive. We'll buy you a car. Eventually, you'll take over the business. We have no sons. And it was a multi-million dollar business. It was very large. And they said, it can all be yours. And I said, gee, that's awfully nice of you. But having survived the war and having paid the price I paid, I really would like to try and get an education. I'd like to go to high school, because I had never been to high school. I was only a youngster in grammar school when it all happened. So I'd like to go to high school. I'd like to learn English, go to high school, go to college, and go to law school. I'd like to be a lawyer. And my aunt in English said to my-- to her husband, she said, that poor child. He lost his mind during the war. Tomorrow, we'll take him to a doctor.
